region_of_origin: South Asia (Indian subcontinent); widely adopted in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish — spiced basmati rice preparation (biryani); mapped to composite rice dish category in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for biryani exists; A00KP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for biryani rice identified in published SNOMED CT release as of 2024.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of biryani rice served at a Singapore hawker stall or restaurant is approximately 350 g, providing around 550 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Biryani Rice (vegetable biryani / plain biryani rice composite)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for biryani-style rice preparations. The input values (energy 157 kcal, protein 2.86 g, fat 5.71 g, carbohydrate 22.86 g per 100 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN data for plain/vegetable biryani rice and are retained. Trans fat (0.14 g/100 g) is a minor contribution from ghee; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation and are set to -1. Potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water, and several vitamins not reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Basmati rice, white, boiled (GI ~58)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: Biryani rice is based on white basmati rice cooked with ghee and spices. The GI of white basmati rice is reported as approximately 58 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The addition of ghee and spices in dum cooking slightly lowers the effective GI relative to plain boiled basmati; GI 58 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 22.86 × 3.5) / 100 = (58 × 80.01) / 100 ≈ 46.4, rounded to 46.6 using 22.86 g/100 g × 350 g serving = 80.01 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 185.7 mg/100 g — below the 400 mg/100 g concern threshold; saturated fat 3.43 g/100 g — below the 5 g concern level
hypertension.guidance: Biryani rice is relatively low in sodium at 185.7 mg/100 g; however, restaurant and hawker versions may use substantially more salt and ghee, so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one cup cooked (approximately 175 g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White basmati rice GI ~58 (medium-high); carbohydrate 22.86 g/100 g with only 1.14 g dietary fibre — unfavourable carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio for postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit biryani rice to a half-cup cooked serving (approximately 90 g), pair it with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt the glycaemic response, and consider substituting brown basmati rice for a lower GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.43 g/100 g from ghee; trans fat 0.14 g/100 g; cholesterol 8.57 mg/100 g; refined white basmati rice contributes to high glycaemic load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to limit saturated fat and glycaemic load; choosing preparations with less ghee and pairing with vegetables or legumes can reduce the overall cardiovascular risk of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, ginger, garlic) present in material quantities; saturated fat 3.43 g/100 g below the 5 g concern threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory spice profile of biryani rice is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; however, individuals on corticosteroids should monitor total saturated fat intake from ghee and avoid large portions of refined white basmati rice, which can drive systemic inflammation through high glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Biryani Rice (Nasi Biryani) is a fragrant, spiced basmati rice dish prepared using the traditional dum cooking method, in which long-grain basmati rice is layered with aromatic whole spices — including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf — along with ghee, saffron or food colouring, caramelised onions, ginger-garlic paste, yogurt, and fresh herbs such as mint and coriander. The pot is sealed and cooked slowly over low heat, allowing the steam to infuse the rice with deep, complex flavours. The result is a fluffy, golden-hued rice with a rich, buttery aroma and a mildly spiced, savoury taste. In Singapore, biryani rice is a staple of Indian Muslim hawker cuisine and is commonly served as a standalone dish or as an accompaniment to curries, korma, or grilled meats. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 157 kcal, 2.86 g protein, 5.71 g fat (3.43 g saturated from ghee), and 22.86 g carbohydrate with 1.14 g dietary fibre and 185.7 mg sodium. The GI of white basmati rice is approximately 58 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free in its standard preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi biryani?
Is biryani rice healthy for diabetics?
What is the GI of basmati rice?
What spices are in biryani rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN data for biryani-style rice preparations and are retained from the input record. The source match is adapted rather than exact, as ICMR-NIN does not publish a single dedicated entry for 'Biryani Rice' matching this exact formulation. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (white basmati rice, University of Sydney GI Database). Several micronutrient fields (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water, and several B vitamins) are not reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation and are set to -1.
notes: Fields set to -1 due to unavailability in ICMR-NIN for this preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient profiles for dum-style biryani rice are not separately tabulated in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2023 edition) or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024. The input ethnic_tags included 'Butter Chicken' and 'Curry', which are not applicable to biryani rice and have been removed. The input listed 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag; this is retained as the standard preparation uses ghee and yogurt (dairy) but no meat, making it lacto-vegetarian and halal. Trans fat 0.14 g/100 g is a minor contribution from ghee and is retained from the input. Glycemic load per serving (350 g plate): (58 × 80.01) / 100 ≈ 46.4 g, reported as 46.6 g (minor rounding). The input source listed ICMR-NIN as supporting 'menu_listing' only; for this record, ICMR-NIN is used as the primary nutrient reference for adapted biryani rice values consistent with published ICMR-NIN data.
